revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:愛染まつり]][[ko:아이젠마쓰리]]
'''Aizen Festival''' (愛染祭, Aizen Matsuri) is a summer festival held annually from June 30 to July 2 at the [[:en:Shitenno-ji|Shitenno-ji]]-affiliated {{ill|Aizen-do Shoman-in|ja|勝鬘院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizen-do Shoman-in|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizen-do Shoman-in|1=Shōman-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11400541}}, commonly known as "Aizen-san," located in {{ill|Tennōji-ku, Osaka|en|Tennōji-ku, Osaka|qid=Q490592|lt=Tennoji Ward, Osaka|1=Tennōji-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.<ref> "Annual Events Encyclopedia" p.1, May 23, 1958, First Edition, Edited by Masayoshi Nishikado, Published by Tokyodo Publishing</ref> This festival is considered one of the "Three Great Summer Festivals of Osaka," alongside {{ill|Tenjin Matsuri|en|Tenjin Matsuri|qid=Q5371810|lt=Tenjin Matsuri|1=Tenjin Matsuri}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Matsuri|ja|住吉祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Matsuri|1=Sumiyoshi Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381874}}, and is hosted by a Buddhist temple. The three festivals are commonly referred to by their nicknames: "Aizen-san," "Tenjin-san," and "Sumiyoshi-san." This has led to the saying, "Osaka’s summer festivals start with Aizen-san and end with Sumiyoshi-san." Some believe this phrase inspired the origin of the expression "Ai-sumi-masen" (a play on "Ai" (love) and "Sumi" (reside)), while others interpret it as a mnemonic device to remember the three festivals: "Ai (Aizen), Sumi (Sumiyoshi), and Masen (Tenjin)." == Overview == The Aizen Festival has been held for over 1,400 years and embodies the Buddhist ideals of {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana Buddhism|1=Mahayana}}, as inherited from {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shotoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}, who sought to bring salvation to those suffering. The festival also serves as a {{ill|Nagoshi no Harae|ja|夏越の大祓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoshi no Harae|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoshi no Harae|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a purification ritual for the summer. On June 30, the head priest of [[:en:Shitenno-ji|Shitenno-ji]] leads the "Nagoshi no Harae Grand Ceremony" at the {{ill|Tahōtō|en|Tahōtō|qid=Q2451601|lt=Tahōtō|1=Tahōtō}}. {{ill|Aizen-do Shoman-in|ja|勝鬘院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizen-do Shoman-in|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aizen-do Shoman-in|1=Shōman-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11400541}}, a sub-temple of [[:en:Shitenno-ji|Shitenno-ji]], was rebuilt under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} and is designated as an Important Cultural Property. The temple is affectionately known as "Aizen-san" due to its enshrined deity, {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|qid=Q1188900|lt=Aizen Myō-ō|1=Rāgarāja}}. The festival began as a gathering where geisha from the entertainment districts rode in palanquins to pray to Aizen Myo-o for blessings. This tradition evolved into the "Hoe Kago" (宝恵駕籠) parade, which continues today, now featuring women in yukata. During the festival, the temple hosts special public viewings of {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|qid=Q1188900|lt=Aizen Myō-ō|1=Rāgarāja}} and {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}, the latter enshrined by Toyotomi Hideyoshi during his Korean campaigns. On June 30, a parade featuring twelve Aizen Maidens (愛染娘) selected annually carries them in seven "Hoe Kago" palanquins, decorated with red and white cloth and "Aizen Katsura" flowers, from {{ill|Tennōji Station|en|Tennōji Station|qid=Q1132328|lt=Tennoji Station|1=Tennōji Station}} to Aizen-do Shoman-in. Upon arrival, the palanquins are lifted high in front of the temple, creating a lively spectacle. Visitors pray for prosperity, love, and charm, receiving "Hana-mamori" (flower amulets) and smiles from the Aizen Maidens. A purification ritual is performed at Osaka’s oldest wooden building, the {{ill|Tahōtō|en|Tahōtō|qid=Q2451601|lt=Tahōtō|1=Tahōtō}}, a National Important Cultural Property. On July 1, the Aizen Maidens showcase their hidden talents in a competition featuring calligraphy, karaoke, cheerleading, and magic tricks. Over 300 food stalls line the festival grounds, attracting large crowds. Since the festival marks the start of summer in Kansai, many visitors wear {{ill|Yukata|en|Yukata|qid=Q849302|lt=Yukata|1=Yukata}}, earning the event the nickname "Yukata Festival." Aizen Maidens distribute "Love Bracelets" (ラブブレス) as a special service, personally placing them on attendees' wrists. Legend states that encountering light rain (nicknamed "Aizen Parapara") on the way to or from the festival ensures that couples will stay together. Another belief suggests that watching a play after attending the festival brings good fortune. == Official Name and Variations == Although formally named "Aizen Matsuri," its spelling varies across promotional materials, appearing as "Aizen Festival," "Aizen Matsuri," "Aizen Mode," and "Shoman Aizenkai." In 2018, the festival was scaled down. While religious ceremonies, deity unveilings, and blessings continued, food stalls and the "Hoe Kago" parade were canceled. This decision aimed to reaffirm the festival’s spiritual purpose. Concerns over noise, reckless motorcycle behavior, illegal parking, littering, and public urination had prompted complaints from local residents, leading to the decision. Moving forward, {{ill|Shoman-in|ja|勝鬘院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoman-in|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoman-in|1=Shōman-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11400541}} plans to discuss with local stakeholders whether to reinstate the parade and festivities in the future.<ref>[https://mainichi.jp/articles/20180509/ddn/041/040/026000c Aizen Festival Scaling Down - Osaka] Mainichi Shimbun, May 8, 2018</ref> == Aizen Maidens == During the festival, selected women promote the event through media appearances and on-site PR activities. Each year, 300-400 applicants enter, with ten finalists chosen through document screening and interviews. Candidates must be cheerful, eager to participate, and available for three days of festival activities. Two international students from {{ill|Seifu Information Academy|ja|清風情報工科学院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu Information Academy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seifu Information Academy|1=Seifu Information Academy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11561471}} join the team, making a total of twelve Aizen Maidens, aged 18 to 35. Their attire consists of yukata provided by Kyoto Koizumi Co., Ltd., adorned with "Aizen Katsura" flowers and a sash reading "Aizen Maidens / Aizen Festival." Recruitment is announced via the festival's official website, local newspapers, and magazines each April. Many former Aizen Maidens pursue careers in entertainment, including {{ill|Hiromi Fujiwara|ja|藤原宏美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromi Fujiwara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiromi Fujiwara|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sumire Seike|ja|清家すみれ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumire Seike|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumire Seike|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aya Kunitake|ja|國武綾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aya Kunitake|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aya Kunitake|1=Aya Kunitake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18817479}}, {{ill|Sayaka Kijima|ja|木島さやか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sayaka Kijima|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sayaka Kijima|1=Sayaka Kijima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11518326}}, and {{ill|Rinko Masuda|ja|増田倫子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinko Masuda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinko Masuda|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 桜 (お笑いコンビ)|1=Sakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537654}}. Politician {{ill|Sayuri Uenishi|en|Sayuri Uenishi|qid=Q11360103|lt=Sayuri Uenishi|1=Sayuri Uenishi}} is also a former Aizen Maiden. Since 2010, an additional "Aizen Women’s Troupe" (愛染女組) of ten members performs PR activities. Unlike the traditional maidens, they wear happi coats and perform lively stage acts, including song and dance. On the festival’s second night, an "Aizen Maiden Contest" determines the year's "Miss Aizen." The winner receives a tiara and represents the festival in public relations until the next year’s event. == Notes == <references /> == External Links == * [http://www.aizendo.com Aizen-do Shoman-in Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|愛染まつり|version=101780907|comment=Imported full ja history}}
